blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: This isn't about Ethan.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: Cassandra—

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: You're scared. You've been scared, and this is what scared looks like when it's had enough. So just — say that. Stop making it about him.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: That's not what I'm—

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Then tell me what it is.
Something broke.

Not loud. Not a shout. Maya just — stopped trying to hold whatever she had been holding since she walked through the door, and for a second her face did something it almost never did in front of Cassandra.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: Do you know what it's like?
Her voice was different now. Stripped of the argument. Just the raw thing underneath.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: Do you know what it's like to wake up in the morning and the first thing your brain does is — check. Is she okay. Has something happened. Is today the day.
She couldn't finish the sentence. She never could finish that sentence.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: I have been doing that since high school. Every morning. Every birthday dinner, every holiday — I sit there looking at you laughing and I think, what if this is the last one. What if this is the last time I hear that laugh. And I don't say it. I don't say it because you don't want to be treated like someone who's almost gone. You want to be treated like someone who's still here. And I do that. I try so hard to do that. But I am carrying this, Cass. I have been carrying it for years and nobody asks me about it because it's your condition. Your life. But it is also mine.
Cassandra had never heard her say that.

She'd known Maya was scared. She'd known it abstractly, the way you know something you have permission to set down when the thought gets too heavy. But she had never heard Maya say this out loud — in plain words, with her jaw tight and her hands trembling and her eyes red in the corners.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Maya—

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: I'm not scared.
Her voice cracked on the last word.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: I'm terrified. Fear is something you can manage. This is — this is just what I live with. Every single day. And now you want to bring someone in. Someone who is going to fall for you the way everyone falls for you. And someday he is going to be sitting in a room somewhere and not know what to do with himself. And I am going to have to help him through it. And I don't know how to do that because I don't know how to do it for myself.
She stopped.
Pressed her lips together.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: I'm not telling you not to love him. I'm not. I just—
A small, quiet break. Not a sob. Not a shout. Just the word splitting open in the middle.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: I don't know how to lose you.
There it was.

Cassandra could have argued with all of it — the fairness to Ethan, the timing, her own right to choose when and how and who. She could have made the case clearly, from first principles, and it would have held.
But she had never heard her sister say that out loud.
And something in the way Maya said it — not as an accusation, not as a demand, just the bare, exhausted truth of it finally spoken into the open — went somewhere that logic couldn't reach.
Cassandra's eyes filled.
She didn't see it coming. She almost never did — she'd learned to feel the pressure building, to notice it arriving, and she could usually redirect before it broke the surface. Not this time.
Maya looked at her. Her own eyes were red.
She sat back down.
For a long time, neither of them said anything.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: I'm sorry.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Don't.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: I didn't mean to—

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: Don't apologize for that.
Cassandra pressed the back of her hand to her eyes. Her breathing was unsteady.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I know what I'm asking you to carry. I've always known.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: I know you have.

blocks.speaker: Cassandra
blocks.content: I don't know how to be sorry for being alive.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: I'm not asking you to be.
A pause. Maya turned her cup on the table. Once. Twice.

blocks.speaker: Maya
blocks.content: I'm just asking you to know that I love you. That's all this ever is. It's just — that.
The kitchen was very quiet.
Eventually Maya said she should go.
Cassandra didn't argue.
